nodes.en: Frameworks and practices for collective decision-making, resource allocation, and conflict resolution—including governance structures, work coordination, and knowledge sharing protocols. Integrates outcomes from infrastructure, tool-making, and medicine into coherent social systems; distinct from technical domains by operating on human coordination rather than material transformation.
nodes.cs: Rámce a postupy pro kolektivní rozhodování, přidělování zdrojů a řešení konfliktů—včetně vládních struktur, koordinace práce a protokolů sdílení znalostí. Integruje výsledky infrastruktury, výroby nástrojů a medicíny do soudržných společenských systémů; odlišuje se od technických domén operováním na lidské koordinaci spíše než hmotné transformaci.
nodes.brief:
Community Organization is the set of frameworks and practices for collective decision-making, resource allocation, and conflict resolution — governance structures, work coordination, and knowledge-sharing protocols — that a rebuilding group needs to keep applying its technical knowledge without collapsing back into disorder. Unlike its siblings it produces no physical output; instead it integrates the results of [[12.01]] Basic Infrastructure, [[12.02]] Tool Making, [[12.03]] Essential Chemistry, and [[12.04]] Basic Medicine Production into a coherent social system, which is why it is sequenced last even though its coordination function operates continuously alongside the other four.

Elsewhere in the corpus, [[04.05]] Political Science studies governance, authority, and collective decision-making as an academic discipline at state and institutional scale. Community Organization is the same subject matter compressed to small-group, post-collapse practice — informal authority, ad hoc dispute resolution, and survival-driven division of labor rather than constitutions and formal state institutions.

Elinor Ostrom's [*Governing the Commons*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governing_the_Commons) documents the design principles that let small groups manage shared resources without central authority or collapse into disorder, directly applicable to a rebuilding community's resource-allocation problem, and Lewis Dartnell's [*The Knowledge*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Knowledge:_How_to_Rebuild_Our_World_From_Scratch) frames why social organization is what keeps rebuilt technical knowledge from being lost again.
